narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles of past students. The model estimated a 64% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the university's bar passage rates. The primary reason for this prediction was your LSAT score of 22, which stands out as the most influential factor pushing the model strongly toward forecasting failure—it's notably lower than the typical score of around 37 seen in the dataset of students who passed. In contrast, your family income bracket provided some positive counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly toward success since upper-middle-class backgrounds are associated with better outcomes in the data. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3 also helped a bit, pulling the prediction toward passing compared to lower performers, though your Year 3 decile of 4 was more average. These top three factors—your LSAT being the dominant driver toward failure, tempered somewhat by family income and early academic decile—overrode other details like your undergraduate GPA of 2.20 (below the average 3.21) and your plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for 93% of students. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile shows solid but not standout early grades and supportive family resources, yet the LSAT gap created a clear risk signal for the model. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer, but a data-driven flag based on historical patterns where low LSATs correlated with bar struggles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or highlighting any upward trends in later deciles might also help. Reach out to admissions advisors for personalized feedback, and explore bridge programs or alternative law schools that weigh holistic factors beyond these metrics. With targeted improvements, you can reapply stronger next cycle.
